data.title: Web 2.0 & Social Media Learning Opporunity
data.content:
Venue: SIMAD University, Mogadishu, Somalia

Date and time: 22- 26 March 2017

Technological innovation is taking place at a breath-taking pace. Simple, open source internet-based applications and services designed to enhance on-line collaboration are now available to the wider public at little or no cost at all. These new online technologies known as Web 2.0 and ‘social media’ enable people to collaborate to create, share and publish information.

With technical support provided by the Technical Centre for Agricultural and Rural Cooperation ACP-EU (CTA), SIMAD University (SU) organizes a ‘Web 2.0 and Social Media Learning Opportunity’ in Mogadishu, Somalia.

Participants will be introduced to selected Web 2.0 and Social Media applications and will learn how to use them hands-on. They will get a chance to see what others have done, get hands-on experience on how to use innovative applications, and assess how they could adopt these innovations within the context of their work and organization.

CTA has a mandate to facilitate access to and dissemination of information in the fields of agriculture and rural development in 78 countries in Africa, the Caribbean and the Pacific (ACP). Supporting the adoption of Web 2.0 and Social Media applications represents a great opportunity for meeting it.
Programme of the Learning Opportunity:

  • Web 2.0 and Social Media: concepts and principles;
  • Selective access to information via (i) advanced multilingual search options; (ii) RSS and (iii) automated alerts;
  • Content curation: Tagging and Social Bookmarking;
  • Photos for the web;
  • Remote collaboration (e.g. GoogleDrive, Dropbox);
  • Online conversations (Skype, Google Hangouts, Viber, Whatsapp);
  • Online mapping (New Google Maps);
  • Blogging (Blogger or WordPress);
  • Micro-blogging (Twitter);
  • Social networking (LinkedIn and Facebook), risks and mitigation measures;
  • Online Communities of Practice;
  • Web 2.0 and Social Media for Agri-business and marketing.
  • Web 2.0 self-instruction (introduction to IMARK)

Who can participate? Eligibility criteria

  • Be computer literate and conversant with browsing the Internet;
  • have regular access to a computer (ownership of a computer is an advantage);
  • be in a position to take along a WIFI-enabled laptop to the training;
  • have an active e-mail account;
  • be able to shoulder the costs of participation to the event (see below)
  • be actively engaged in agriculture and rural development: political value chains and agricultural trade, etc. (primary target)
  • be resident in Somalia

Participation of women is strongly encouraged.

Course fee

Course fee = 30 USD (10 USD for registration and 20 USD for certificates) payable in advance.

The fee is meant to cover the costs incurred by SIMAD University in the preparation of the event. The cost also includes the provision of refreshments during the training.

Responsibilities

In addition to paying the course fee, participants will be responsible for all costs related to their travel and accommodation to and from the training venue located in SIMAD University. Participants are also required to bring their own WIFI-enabled laptop.

SIMAD University is responsible for the organization of the event. As a partner, CTA provides technical support, ensures monitoring and quality control and offers trainees the opportunity to join online communities of peers.

Entitlements

During each event, SIMAD University will provide refreshments and distribute copies of CTA publications and instructional material.

Registration procedure and deadline

Interested people have to register via this link. Registration will be confirmed after completion of the online questionnaire and payment of the course fee. The organizers will get individually in touch with registrants and provide information on how to pay the course fee and join the event.

Deadline for registration: March 15, 2017

Contact (for clarifications)

SIMAD University

Mr. Husein Osman Abdullahi

Deputy Dean (Admission & Record)

Telephone: +252616774771

Email : husein@simad.edu.so

Follow web2forDev on LinkedIn and DGroups and join the community on Twitter, Facebook

data.title: Appointment of New Rector at SIMAD University
data.content:
SIMAD University is pleased to announce the appointment of Dr. Abdikarim Mohaidin Ahmed as the new Rector of SIMAD University, effective August 2024. Dr. Mohaidin, who was officially appointed by the University's Board of Trustees, has today assumed his role following a formal handover ceremony held at the Town Campus.

Dr. Mohaidin brings with him a wealth of experience and a strong track record in academic leadership, having previously served in various key positions at the University. These include Deputy Rector for Institutional Development, Director for International Cooperation, and Dean of the Faculty of Computing, among others.

As the 4th Rector of SIMAD University, Dr. Mohaidin is committed to furthering the University's mission of providing quality education and driving socioeconomic development in Somalia. His leadership will focus on enhancing academic excellence, fostering innovation, and promoting sustainability.

The University community extends its heartfelt congratulations to Dr. Mohaidin on his new role and looks forward to continued growth and success under his leadership.

data.title: Quality at the Helm: Charting Somali Universities into East Africa’s Academic Mainstream
data.content:
‘The time is always right to do what is right’ as alluded by Martin Luther King Jr. It has now been nearly a month since the Cabinet announced the formation of a 15-member council for higher education in Somalia. There have been congratulatory messages on various social media platforms. The initial excitement has faded, laying bare the enormity of the task and the unforgiving scrutiny it will attract, and the appointed members should be warming up to face the hurdle ahead of them. ‘We must accept finite disappointment but never lose infinite hope’.  I am sure this council understands that it is responsible for higher education in a fragmented country.  A higher education landscape that has grown at breakneck speed, swelling from one public institution before 1991 to more than a hundred, chiefly private universities, enrolling over 150,000 students nationwide. This rapid expansion highlights the demand for post-secondary education in a country where two-thirds of the population is under forty years old. However, it also reveals a lack of consistent quality assurance. Given the failure of the interim council formed in 2019 to fulfil its mandate, the urgency of constituting the higher education commission was apparent. In this context, the Ministry of Education, Culture and Higher Education (MoECHE) deserves genuine applause for shepherding the National Commission for Higher Education (NCHE) draft bill to cabinet ratification on 10 July 2025. By securing unanimous approval for a 15-member oversight body, the ministry signalled that universities and colleges are no longer peripheral to recovery but central to state-building. This move is expected to enhance educational standards and make a significant contribution to national development.

The timing is equally striking as the commission emerges barely sixteen months after Somalia deposited its instrument of ratification to join the East African Community (EAC) on 4 March 2024. Somalia must meet CHEA quality standards for higher education, as required of all member states. Therefore, while the establishment of the Somali Higher Education Commission is a cause for recognition, it should also be approached with careful consideration and practical awareness. ‘courage is not the absence of fear, but rather the assessment that something else is more important than fear’. Over the past 30 years, conflict, chronic underfunding, and the rapid expansion of private universities have all had a significant impact on Somali higher education. Many institutions lack effective quality control and have too few lecturers to meet demand. Academic programs often prioritise immediate market demands over long-term national goals. Some universities in Somalia operate with less than 60% of their required faculty, placing immense strain on staff and likely diminishing academic quality and institutional credibility. To address these faculty shortages and uphold educational standards, the Higher Education Commission (HEC) must exercise its full regulatory authority. Articles 19–20 of the national higher education law grant the HEC the legal mandate to enforce compliance through licensing, accreditation, disciplinary actions, and the publication of binding regulations. When equipped with these statutory "teeth," the HEC can move beyond fragmented oversight and work to transform the current patchwork of institutions into a coherent national higher education framework aligned with the East African Community's Common Higher Education Area.

The Higher Education Commission (HEC) is the national regulatory body for higher education in Somalia. With ongoing political support and steady funding, it can change the sector from a fragmented, survival-oriented system into a unified force that brings people together for national development and regional integration. The HEC is crucial for ensuring that quality is consistent, that universities are held accountable, and that Somali universities align with regional frameworks, such as the East African Community's Common Higher Education Area. To achieve this, key steps include making credit transferable, making enrollment and academic performance data publicly available, ensuring that federal and state agencies collaborate to oversee these processes, and clearly defining quality standards. The Bologna Process in the European Union is an example of how credit portability has been successfully implemented, making it easier for students to move around and have their degrees recognised in all member states. A similar approach in Somalia could make Somali degrees much more credible and comparable. If Somalia implements these changes, it can transform its higher education system into a reliable and unifying institution. This change is necessary to restore public trust, establish a common national goal, and move forward with regional integration in a federal system that is still in its development phase.

Somalia’s Higher Education Commission (HEC) is young and operating in a fragile, devolved system, yet it must still reassure the public that universities are properly regulated. The quickest route is not to invent new rules but to enforce the good ones that already exist especially Article 19 of Somalia’s Higher Education Law, which lets the HEC license, inspect and, when necessary, close non‑compliant universities.

Kenya shows how visible enforcement builds trust. Its Commission for University Education routinely publishes closure notices and has shuttered at least 15 illegal campuses. It also runs a live, searchable register of accredited institutions a model Somalia could emulate with a quarterly digital dashboard that lists authorised universities, flags “ghost” campuses and lets students confirm accreditation in real time. Transparency makes regulation credible. Uganda proves that steady, independent funding sustains that credibility. A small student levy of UGX 20,000 (about US $5) yields almost US $900 000 a year, a portion of which is ring‑fenced for audits and programme reviews. Because Article 22 of Somalia’s Higher Education Act already allows the HEC to retain non‑tax revenues, a modest 0.5 percent tuition surcharge could keep inspectors in the field even when the treasury is empty. Rwanda adds the incentive lesson: only fully accredited universities may compete for national research grants. Tying donor or diaspora funds to compliance would similarly turn oversight from a burden into a prize.

Finally, Somalia need not reinvent academic tools. The Inter‑University Council for East Africa offers off‑the‑shelf curricula, credit‑transfer systems and audit handbooks, while the East African Quality Assurance Network pairs new regulators with seasoned mentors. A digital‑first mindset online license application, remote degree verification and secure whistle‑blower portals would further cut costs and allow continuous supervision, even in regions where travel is risky.

There is need to enforce existing powers visibly, funding its work sustainably, rewarding institutions that follow the rules and borrowing proven regional resources, the HEC can anchor credibility, openness and resilience at the heart of Somalia’s higher‑education system doing small things in a great way, until greater things become possible.

Somalia’s new Higher Education Commission (HEC) can win trust quickly by fusing Nigeria’s and Ethiopia’s proven models. Nigeria’s National Universities Commission pairs six‑year self‑studies with peer review and public league tables, a formula that pushed the University of Lagos to a 20 percent graduation‑rate jump in five years. Ethiopia’s HERQA keeps the self‑study culture but adds phased audits and the power to suspend rogue campuses. Adopting both state‑level “quality desks” for first reviews, federal HEC for final licences and closures gives Somalia rigour without throttling autonomy.

The legal base already exists. Article 10 links the HEC to ministry policy, yet Article 9(2) grants full corporate autonomy. A Mogadishu secretariat can service a 15‑member board that meets monthly and delegates specialist work, while two‑person desks in every federal state provide eyes on the ground once a PhD‑level executive director is in post.

Four tools make the system run: an open‑data platform for quarterly enrolment, staff and finance uploads; a draft National Qualifications Framework within nine months, final by month twelve; one accreditation code aligned with Inter‑University Council for East Africa standards; and a Recognition of Prior Learning scheme that converts informal skills into credit under Article 45.

Stable money and radical transparency seal legitimacy. A modest domestic levy can replace donor volatility, and a trilingual dashboard must publish all income and accreditation results each quarter. Toll‑free SMS whistle‑blower lines, geo‑tagged video inspections and rapid ten‑day “Blue‑Team” audits extend oversight into insecure regions. Early wins are crucial: publish a National Tertiary Atlas by month six, finish sixty peer‑mentored audits by month eighteen, and secure at least one IUCEA‑recognised university per state within two years. By centralising standards, decentralising voice and delivering quick results, the HEC can anchor a credible, resilient and regionally respected Somali higher‑education system.                

Call to Action: Transforming Somalia’s Higher Education Governance

From Law to Implementation

Somalia’s Parliament has passed Higher Education act, establishing the National Council for Higher Education (NCHE) as the apex body to lead, regulate, and oversee all universities and colleges. This law gives the HEC broad authority to set nationwide standards and enforce rules to assure quality, and it is empowered to operate with autonomy and generate its own revenue to fund its activities. However, passing the law is only the first step. The real challenge now is to operationalize the HEC and implement a robust regulatory framework in a country where higher education has long been fragmented and largely unregulated after decades of conflict. There is no time to lose; moving from law-on-paper to law-in-action is a high-stakes sprint that will determine whether Somalia’s universities improve or continue to proliferate without standards. The government has signaled its commitment by appointing a 15-member national higher education committee under this law – a milestone that “lays the foundation for a more standardized, transparent, and effective regulatory framework” in the post-conflict era. Now, concrete actions are urgently needed to turn that promise into reality.

Immediate Steps to Activate the HEC

To get the HEC up and running, several critical actions should be taken immediately:

  1. Appoint Qualified Leadership: Appoint a qualified Executive Director to head the Higher Education Council (HEC). Mirroring international practice, this officer should manage the secretariat and report to the board. Once confirmed, the Director will establish a secretariat, coordinate with institutions, and oversee start‑up operations, enabling the HEC to become fully operational.
  2. The Council should develop a comprehensive five‑year strategic plan that articulates a clear roadmap for its work and identifies the initiatives to be prioritized during this period and beyond
  3. Establish an Online Licensing Portal: The HEC secretariat should deploy a single digital portal for all licensing and accreditation submissions. Centralisation will replace the current ad hoc process, heighten transparency, enable real time tracking, and reduce administrative burden. This e governance measure signals a service-oriented regulator and guarantees equitable access for institutions across Somalia’s federal states
  4. Secure Start Up Funding: Launch the NCHE with federal seed appropriations for staffing, premises, and initial inspections. Sustain its operations through a 0.5 % tuition levy complemented by government co funding, replicating Uganda’s blended model. This diversified revenue stream insulates essential oversight inspections, audits, data management from fiscal shortfalls and underpins long term quality assurance.

Year One: Establishing a Credible Regulatory Framework

During its first fiscal year, the   Higher Education Council (HEC) should lay the groundwork for an effective oversight system. The top priority is to issue a unified national accreditation code based on the Inter‑University Council for East Africa’s quality‑assurance benchmarks. Because Somalia has joined the East African Community, aligning with these regional standards will provide universities a clear blueprint for raising academic quality and will reassure students, employers, and international partners that Somali degrees meet recognized norms.

Concurrently, the HEC should introduce time‑limited provisional licences for promising institutions that have not yet met every requirement for full accreditation. Echoing Kenya’s “Letter of Interim Authority,” this status allows universities to operate under closer supervision while they strengthen faculty, facilities, and governance. By the close of year one, each reputable institution should hold either a full or provisional license; any that do not risk being declared illegal a decisive step toward bringing an unregulated sector under control.

To reinforce transparency, the Council must also launch a trilingual (Somali, English, Arabic) online dashboard that displays each university’s licensing status, fees, and any sanctions in real time. This public portal will enable students, parents, and employers to verify a university’s standing instantly, deter misconduct, and rebuild trust in higher education. Together, clear standards, phased licensing, and public accountability will mark the end of “paper universities” and usher in a culture of quality.

Year Two: Deep Audits and Regional Integration

In its second year, the NCHE should pivot from institution‑building to rigorous, evidence‑based quality assurance and regional recognition. It must oversee approximately sixty peer‑reviewed audits enough to evaluate every Somali university conducted by experienced academics from both Somalia and neighboring countries. The results will inform Somalia’s inaugural “State of Higher Education” report to Parliament and the public, spotlighting systemic gaps, recommending remedies, and initiating sanctions where standards remain unmet. By the end of the year, policymakers will hold a comprehensive baseline against which future progress can be measured.

At the same time, the Council should help at least one university in every federal member state earn accreditation that meets Inter‑University Council for East Africa standards. Pairing these institutions with mentor campuses elsewhere in the region will smooth the accreditation process, encourage staff and student exchanges, and raise the standing of Somali degrees. Securing six such accreditations, one per state will instill a nationwide culture of excellence, show that reforms are producing real results, and set a clear benchmark for the rest of the sector.

Toward Rule‑Based, Resilient Governance

Meeting the Year One and Year Two milestones will recast Somalia’s higher‑education governance from fragmented to coherent. A fully operational Higher Education Council, working in concert with state ministries, will delineate responsibilities: the Council sets national standards and issues licences, while states oversee implementation. This clear division eliminates overlap and streamlines decision‑making. The reforms also activate a five‑pillar quality‑assurance architecture institutional licensing, programme accreditation, data disclosure, peer‑review audits, and proportionate sanctions. Together these mechanisms replace discretionary judgments with transparent, evidence‑based rules, aligning Somalia with international regulatory norms. Over time, weak programmes will be upgraded or closed, students will be shielded from fraud, and foreign partners will view Somali institutions as credible collaborators.

Financial sustainability is equally critical. A diversified revenue mixes central allocations, state contributions, and a modest student surcharge will free the Council from ad‑hoc donor funding and ensure it can maintain inspections, databases, and public reporting. Publishing audited accounts will reinforce public and donor confidence that resources are used efficiently.

Law No. 30 and the creation of the Higher Education Council mark a beginning, not an end. Urgent, disciplined implementation competent leadership, robust tools, and transparent audits can shift the sector from laissez‑faire to merit‑based excellence. The payoff will be graduates with trusted qualifications, institutions that match regional peers, and a workforce equipped to drive national development. The opportunity is open; decisive action now will secure lasting change.

Conclusion

The swift operationalisation of the National Council for Higher Education is no mere administrative checkpoint; it is the definitive measure of Somalia’s resolve to deliver internationally benchmarked quality assurance and systemic coherence. A fully empowered HEC grounded in Law No. 30 and aligned with the East African Community’s Common Higher Education Area must secure early, visible wins: licensing all credible institutions, executing rigorous audits, and attaining Inter‑University Council for East Africa recognition for at least one university in every federal state. These milestones will restore public faith in degree validity, assure employers of graduate competence, and signal to partners and donors that Somalia now possesses a transparent, rule‑based regulatory architecture worthy of sustained investment.

Financial and institutional resilience are equally indispensable. A diversified revenue portfolio central allocation, state contributions, and a modest tuition levy will fund continuous inspections, robust data systems, and public reporting, insulating oversight from fiscal shocks. Systematic publication of accreditation outcomes, audited accounts, and performance metrics will institutionalise accountability and cultivate a culture of evidence‑driven enhancement across the sector. Executed with urgency, professionalism, and scholarly rigour, the NCHE can become the vital conduit between campuses and cabinet, state ministries and federal policy, and Somalia’s universities and the wider East African knowledge economy. In doing so, it will transform a fragmented landscape into a coherent national qualification’s framework anchored in equity, excellence, and perpetual improvement attributes essential to powering Somalia’s long‑term socio‑economic resurgence and regional integration. Whether higher education can soar, stride, or advance only in measured increments, the imperative is unchanging: its momentum must never cease.

 

Dr. Abukar Mukhtar Omar

Dr. Abukar Mukhtar Omar is the Dean of the Graduate School at SIMAD University in Mogadishu, Somalia, and an expert on quality assurance in higher education.  He earned his BSc in Education from the Islamic University in Uganda, followed by both a Master of Arts in Educational Policy and Planning and a PhD in Education Policy, Planning and Management from Makerere University, Uganda. Dr. Abukar is East African Quality Assurance’s (EAQAN) representative for Somalia. His research focuses on higher‑education policy, governance, and quality assurance.

data.title: SIMAD University Launches Strategic Plan 2026–2030
data.content:
SIMAD University has officially launched its Strategic Plan 2026–2030, introducing a comprehensive roadmap designed to guide the institution’s growth, innovation, and institutional transformation over the next five years.

The new strategy positions SIMAD University as a forward-looking institution committed to strengthening entrepreneurial education, research and innovation, digital transformation, and global partnerships while enhancing its contribution to national development.

As higher education institutions worldwide adapt to technological change and evolving workforce demands, SIMAD’s strategic framework reflects a clear commitment to preparing students for the future while expanding the university’s impact on society.

Key Strategic Priorities

The SIMAD Strategic Plan 2026–2030 is built around five core priorities that will shape the university’s direction in the coming years.

Entrepreneurial Education

SIMAD University aims to develop graduates who possess not only academic knowledge but also the entrepreneurial mindset and practical skills necessary to innovate, create opportunities, and contribute to economic growth.

Research, Innovation, and Sustainability

The university is expanding its focus on research that addresses real-world challenges, including sustainable development, public health, economic resilience, and governance.

Digital Transformation

Digital innovation remains a central pillar of SIMAD’s strategy. Investments in technology, digital learning systems, and data-driven decision-making will enhance both teaching and institutional management.

People and Partnerships

SIMAD continues to strengthen its academic community by investing in faculty development, empowering students, and building strong partnerships with international universities, research institutions, and development organizations.

Institutional Effectiveness

The strategy also focuses on strengthening governance, accountability, and performance frameworks to ensure effective implementation and long-term institutional sustainability.

From Strategy to Implementation

To translate the Strategic Plan into actionable initiatives, SIMAD University organized a Strategic Plan Implementation Retreat that brought together senior university leadership, including deputy rectors, deans, and directors.

During the retreat, participants developed implementation frameworks, divisional action plans, and performance indicators to guide the execution of the strategy across all departments.

Leadership sessions on adaptive leadership and institutional change also helped strengthen the university’s readiness to implement the strategy effectively.

A Vision for the Future of Higher Education

The launch of the Strategic Plan 2026–2030 represents SIMAD University’s continued commitment to innovation, academic excellence, and societal impact.

By strengthening its research capacity, expanding digital learning, and fostering strategic partnerships, SIMAD aims to prepare graduates who can lead and contribute meaningfully to the development of Somalia and the global knowledge economy.

With this strategic roadmap in place, SIMAD University continues to position itself as a leading center for higher education, innovation, and research in the region.

data.title: SIMAD University Expands Global Academic Partnerships in Malaysia
data.content:
SIMAD University has taken another significant step in advancing its internationalisation agenda through a high-level strategic visit to Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia, aimed at strengthening global academic partnerships, expanding postgraduate opportunities, and enhancing research and professional collaboration. The visit, which took place from 5–15 May 2026, brought together senior university leadership, international academic institutions, development partners, and postgraduate students in a series of engagements focused on higher education innovation and capacity development.

The SIMAD University delegation was led by Mr. Abdullahi Ahmed Abdirahman, Deputy Rector for Finance and Administration, and Dr. Abukar Mukhtar Omar, Dean of the Graduate School, accompanied by eleven graduating students from the SIMAD–Open University Malaysia (OUM) postgraduate programmes.

Strengthening the SIMAD–OUM Strategic Partnership

A key component of the visit was the strategic management meeting between SIMAD University and Open University Malaysia (OUM), one of SIMAD’s longest-standing international academic partnerships. For over 13 years, the collaboration has delivered postgraduate programmes in Master of Business Administration (MBA) and Master of Project Management (MPM), benefiting Somali professionals across both public and private sectors.

The discussions focused on repositioning the SIMAD–OUM partnership from a traditional programme delivery model into a broader strategic academic alliance centred on research collaboration, innovation, quality assurance, faculty exchange, student mobility, and professional development.

Both institutions also agreed to formalise the expanded collaboration through a revised Memorandum of Understanding (MoU), reflecting their shared commitment to advancing higher education and human capital development in Somalia.

Celebrating Academic Excellence at the OUM Convocation Ceremony

As part of the visit, postgraduate students from SIMAD University Graduate School participated in the OUM Convocation Ceremony held at the World Trade Centre Kuala Lumpur on 8–9 May 2026. The ceremony marked a proud milestone for the graduates and highlighted the continued success of the SIMAD–OUM partnership in expanding access to internationally recognised postgraduate education for Somali professionals.

Expanding Development Partnerships with NAMA Foundation

The delegation also visited NAMA Foundation to explore collaboration opportunities in education financing, scholarships, youth empowerment, and community development. Discussions included the progress of the Qard Hassan Programme, which supports undergraduate students through educational financing initiatives managed in partnership with DirectAid and Yayasan Educational Aid International.

Both parties expressed strong interest in strengthening cooperation through sustainable institutional partnerships focused on higher education support, professional development, and community impact initiatives in Somalia.

New Partnership Opportunities with IIUM and USIM

As part of its broader international engagement strategy, SIMAD University held strategic meetings with the International Islamic University Malaysia (IIUM) and Universiti Sains Islam Malaysia (USIM).

At IIUM’s Institute of Islamic Banking and Finance (IIiBF), discussions focused on launching internationally recognised Islamic Banking Professional Certification programmes and Professional PhD programmes through SIMAD Graduate School. SIMAD University will serve as the official resource centre for these programmes in Somalia, creating greater access to globally recognised qualifications for professionals in the banking and finance sector.

Meanwhile, discussions with USIM centred on future collaboration in joint research, postgraduate programme development, academic exchange, faculty and student mobility, and international academic conferences. Both institutions agreed to advance discussions toward a formal MoU that will support long-term institutional cooperation.

Advancing SIMAD University’s Global Vision

The Malaysia visit concluded with a clear roadmap for strengthening SIMAD University’s international academic profile and expanding opportunities for students, researchers, and professionals in Somalia. The visit also reinforced the university’s strategic commitment to global partnerships, research excellence, innovation, and high-quality postgraduate education.

Through these strategic engagements, SIMAD University continues to position itself as a leading institution in higher education, international collaboration, and professional capacity development in Somalia and across the region.
